---
title: "GavinK_Comments"
output: html_document
---

Below contains some **specific things I noticed while performing the code-fold audit.** I have organized my thoughts by section (and only included sections where I noticed something).

See `reviewer-GavinK-CodeAudit.Rmd` for the code-fold audit itself.

One note: there were a number of places were I thought `ggplot2` could be formatted like it is elsewhere (i.e., \textcolor{red}{\textbf{ggplot2}}). I stopped listing these midway through chapter 4 as I was unsure if this was intentional and I was missing something. If this was not intentional, I am happy to edit instances of "ggplot2" for consistency.

# Chapter 3: Getting Started

## 3.1 --- Why plot your data?

### 3.1.2 --- One lousy point can ruin your day

> In this example, it was arguable that x and y axes should be reversed, to determine how well measured weight can be predicted from reported weight. In ggplot this can easily be done by reversing the x and y aesthetics.

- `ggplot` can be changed to ggplot2 and formatted the same as it is elsewhere (e.g., bold + red).

# Chapter 4: Plots of Multivariate Data

## 4.1 --- Bivariate summaries

### 4.1.1 --- Smoothers

> Non-parametric regression is a broad topic; see Fox (2016), Ch. 18 for a more general treatment including smoothing splines, and Wood (2006) for generalized additive models, fit using method = "gam" in ggplot2, which is the default when the largest group has more than 1,000 observations.

- 'ggplot2' here is bolded but not coloured red

### 4.1.2 --- Stratifiers

> In ggplot2 thinking, grouping is accomplished simply by adding an aesthetic, such as color = rank.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

> The story in Figure 4.5 is again different. Faculty in applied disciplines on average earn about 10,000$ more per year on average than their theoretical colleagues.

- 10,000\$ -> \$10,000

- "on average" appears twice

> For both groups, there is an approximately linear relation up to about 30–40 years, but the smoothed curves then diverge into the region where the data is thinner.

- Group `B: Applied` seems to plateau between 20-30 years

## 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

> 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

- "Ellipses" -> ellipses

### 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

> 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

- Capitalization after the colon is inconsistent with other labels (e.g., "4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships")

## 4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships

***Label: fig-peng-simpsons***

- This may be one of the figures Reviewer 1 was referring to as "too small"

> The ggplot2 code for the panels in this figure are shown below. 

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

## 4.6 --- Multivariate normality and outliers

### 4.6.2 Penguin data

> It is a bit tricky to label points selectively in ggplot2 when the criterion for which points to label is complex, or involves variables outside the data frame.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

***Code that defines*** `peng.mod`:

- Chunk unlabeled, formatting of code does not appear ideally in the HTML version.

- This is *not* the chunk in the "Looking Ahead" callout in 4.7 

## 4.7 --- Scatterplot matrices

>  (The default colors are somewhat garish, so I use scales::hue_pal() to mimic the discrete color scale used in ggplot2).

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

#### Looking Ahead

- `peng.lda` code has same formatting issues as the mentioned chunk containing `peng.mod` (see 4.6.2)

## 4.9 --- Generalized pairs plots

#### The species/sex/island penguin `pairs()` plot

***Label: fig-peng-mosaic***

- Species labels overlap. Perhaps fix by rotating labels?

## 4.14 --- Exercises

> 7. Other implementations of parallel coordinate plots in R include: MASS::parcoord(), GGally::ggparcoord() and PairViz::pcp(). The ggpcp version used here is the most general.

- ggpcp not coloured red like other packages

# Chapter 5: Dimension Reduction

# Appendix B --- R Code for Figures and Analyses

- Some of the labelling is inconsistent, e.g., '--' vs. ':' in the below:

> Davis data-- Models and plots
> Salaries data: scatterplots

- Davis-reg.R is also listed twice: once under Chapter 3 and once under Rcode

- Capitalization of first letter after emdash inconsistent, e.g.:

> levdemo.R --- Leverage and influence demo
> hatvalues-demo.R --- leverage and the data ellipse

<!-- I have read up to the start of chapter 5 -->
